summary:
The Battle of Stalingrad, fought from August 1942 to February 1943, was the turning point of [[event:112|World War II]] on the Eastern Front and one of the bloodiest battles in human history. It ended with the encirclement and destruction of Germany's Sixth Army — the most powerful force in the Wehrmacht — and marked the last major German offensive in the east. Total casualties on both sides exceeded two million killed, wounded, or captured, making it the single deadliest battle of the entire war.

Hitler ordered the capture of Stalingrad partly for its strategic value as an industrial city controlling the Volga River and partly for symbolic reasons — the city bore Stalin's name. The German advance reached the city in September 1942, and the subsequent fighting became some of the most brutal urban combat ever recorded. Soviet forces under General Vasily Chuikov's 62nd Army clung to narrow strips of the Volga bank, exploiting the close-quarter fighting to negate German air and artillery superiority. Snipers, building-by-building fighting, and almost daily counterattacks turned the city into a grinding charnel house for both sides. German forces captured approximately 90 percent of the city but could not eliminate Soviet resistance.

The decisive stroke came on 19 November 1942, when Soviet forces launched Operation Uranus — a massive pincer attack against the weakly held Romanian flanks of the German salient. Within four days, the Sixth Army of some 330,000 men was encircled. Hitler forbade withdrawal, insisting the army hold as a "fortress." A German relief attempt (Operation Winter Storm) was turned back, and an air supply operation proved wholly inadequate. The starving, frostbitten remnants of the Sixth Army surrendered in early February 1943; approximately 91,000 survivors entered Soviet captivity, from which fewer than 6,000 ever returned.

Stalingrad's strategic consequences were enormous. It shattered German offensive capability in the east and began a Soviet advance that — with setbacks at [[event:2761|Kursk]] — would carry Soviet forces to Berlin by 1945. The battle also deeply affected German morale: for the first time, Hitler's propaganda machine could not disguise a catastrophic defeat. In the occupied territories and among Germany's allies, Stalingrad signaled that the war might not be won. The Soviet Union's ability to plan and execute such a complex operation demonstrated military sophistication that German planners had fatally underestimated.
